title: Make Something on a Screen
developmental_frame: At 9–10, after two quarters of hands-on making, a first *digital* creation is exciting and doable with kid-friendly tools — a slideshow story, a digital comic, a simple animation. Keep it small and purposeful, and hold the same rule as always: understand what you make and stay the boss of the tool. This is the gentle on-ramp to the 'Build' skills that grow through the later years, introduced without drowning them in screens.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is the thrill of a new kind of making — 'I made a real thing on a screen, and it's mine.' The same pride as a hand-made object, now in a digital shape someone can view and enjoy.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child makes their first real digital creation and shows it to someone who enjoys it. The novelty of making on a screen, combined with the familiar pride of finishing something real, is highly motivating — and doing it with the 'I understand every part' rule means they feel like a maker, not just a button-presser.

artifact: One simple digital creation (a short slideshow story, a digital drawing or comic, or a simple animation) made with a kid-friendly tool and viewed by a real person.
success_criteria: The child planned the digital thing before making it.
They can explain what every part of it is and how they made it — nothing is a mystery button.
A real person viewed it and the child improved one thing based on the reaction.

lessons.title: You can make on a screen too
lessons.concept: You've made things with your hands — now you can make things on a screen: a story slideshow, a comic, a little animation. It's the same idea (make a real thing someone enjoys), just with a digital tool instead of glue and cardboard.
lessons.real_example: A slideshow that tells the story of your weekend, with your own drawings or photos on each slide, is a real digital creation — and you can share it with family far away.
lessons.apply_task: Pick one small digital thing to make and who it's for. Say it: 'I'll make a ___ for ___.'
lessons.reflection_prompt: Is your digital idea small enough to finish? Same rule as hand-making: small and finished beats big and abandoned.
lessons.feedback_task: Tell an adult your idea and ask them to help pick a simple, safe kid-friendly tool for it.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to a sibling that making on a screen follows the same steps as making by hand.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: A grown-up should help you choose a safe tool. And remember: the *idea* is yours — the tool just helps you make it. You're the maker, not the tool.
success_criteria: A small digital creation and its audience are chosen.

lessons.title: Plan it first (yes, still!)
lessons.concept: Making on a screen doesn't skip the planning. Sketch your slides or scenes on paper first — what's on each one, in what order. A paper plan makes the screen part quick and calm instead of confusing.
lessons.real_example: Before touching the computer, comic-makers draw tiny boxes on paper showing each scene. Then the digital part is just 'copy my plan onto the screen' — much easier.
lessons.apply_task: Sketch your digital thing on paper — each slide, scene, or part, in order.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Did planning on paper first make the idea clearer? It almost always does.
lessons.feedback_task: Show your paper plan to someone and check the order makes sense before you build.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show a friend why planning on paper first makes screen-making easier.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Plan with your own hands on paper — this is your thinking. Doing it yourself keeps you the boss of the whole project.
success_criteria: A paper plan of the digital creation exists, in order.

lessons.title: Make it — and understand every part
lessons.concept: Now build your digital thing from your plan. The golden rule: understand every part you add. If a tool (or AI) makes something for you, make sure you know what it is and could explain it — otherwise it's not really yours.
lessons.real_example: If you use a tool to add a picture, you should be able to say 'I chose that picture and put it there because…' If you can't explain a part, you're not the maker of it yet.
lessons.apply_task: Make your digital creation from your plan. For each part, be ready to say what it is and why it's there.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Is there any part you can't explain? If so, learn it or change it — a maker understands their own work.
lessons.feedback_task: Show a parent the thing as you build and have them point to any 'mystery part' you can't explain. Go understand it.
lessons.teach_back_task: Walk someone through your digital creation, explaining each part in your own words.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: If AI helps you make a part (like a picture), you must still understand and choose it — **never keep a part you can't explain.** A maker who can't explain their work is just copying; you're better than that.
success_criteria: The child can explain every part in their own words.

lessons.title: Show it and make it better
lessons.concept: Show your creation to a real viewer and watch how they react. Where they smile, where they look confused — that tells you what to keep and what to fix. Then improve one thing. Same as hand-making: real viewers make your work better.
lessons.real_example: You show your slideshow story and your viewer looks lost on slide 3. That's your fix — maybe slide 3 needs to be clearer or split in two.
lessons.apply_task: Show your creation to a real person, stay quiet, and note where they react or get confused. Improve the most confusing part.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Was the confusing part one you were proud of? Making for a viewer is different from making for yourself.
lessons.feedback_task: After improving, show it again. Did the fix work?
lessons.teach_back_task: Teach someone the 'show it, watch quietly, fix what confused them' rule for digital things too.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: A real human viewer's reaction is the best feedback — a screen can't be delighted or confused the way a person can. Trust the real reaction.
success_criteria: A real person viewed the creation.
One improvement was made from real reaction and re-checked.
